title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 45-3
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Ontario. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for starting the discussion. For an opening proposal, I can supply at 2.36 per unit, which reflects current cost pressure while still leaving room to move quickly if volume and commitment are solid. If you can indicate the expected volume and schedule reliability, I may be able to work with you on structure. As the supplier opening the negotiation, I want to anchor near the upper end of the apparent market range while preserving room for concessions. This price is above my minimum threshold and supports the objective of keeping volume committed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.25.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening proposal. At 2.36 we're very close to the top of what we can make work on our side, especially once downstream margin and execution risk are factored in. If volume is stable and the delivery schedule is dependable, I can move quickly at 2.22 per unit as a serious counterproposal. The supplier's opening price is below my maximum buy threshold, but it's still high relative to my margin objective. A lower counteroffer tests room to move while keeping the discussion alive early in the negotiation.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.22, below its hidden seller floor of 2.65.

This price is above my minimum threshold and supports the objective of keeping volume committed.

steps.message: I appreciate the serious counterproposal and the emphasis on stable volume and dependable scheduling. I can narrow the gap and come down to 2.31 per unit, which reflects a real concession from our side while still covering supply execution risk and keeping the program viable. If you can hold to the volume expectations and keep the delivery cadence firm, this is a price I can move forward on quickly. The buyer is below our last offer but still above our reservation floor, so there is room to trade without conceding too much too early. A midpoint-style counter keeps momentum, signals flexibility, and preserves margin while testing whether the buyer is prepared to close.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement to 2.31. I can tighten my side and come up to 2.26 per unit if we pair that with the steady volume and firm delivery cadence we discussed. That is a meaningful concession from my last position and gets us closer to something I can support while still protecting the program economics on my end. The supplier's 2.31 offer is within my buy ceiling of 2.39, so a deal is possible, but there is still room to negotiate lower and preserve more downstream margin. With limited rounds left, a moderate counteroffer keeps momentum without giving away too much too quickly.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.26, below its hidden seller floor of 2.65.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.26 and the emphasis on steady volume and delivery discipline. To close the remaining gap, I can come down to 2.29 per unit if we lock in the committed volume and the firm cadence we discussed, which keeps the program workable on my side while recognizing your concession. That narrows this to a very small difference and puts us in a realistic position to finalize quickly this round. The buyer is below the last seller offer but still above my minimum sell threshold, so there is room to keep negotiating rather than accept immediately. A modest counter at 2.29 preserves margin, signals flexibility, and stays close enough to the buyer's position to maintain momentum.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: We can proceed at $2.27 per unit, provided the committed volume and firm delivery cadence are locked in as discussed. You're very close to our last position, and this closes the gap at a level we can support while keeping the program moving without further delay. steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. We can start at 2.92 per unit based on current input costs and the need to keep the program commercially workable on our side. If there's room for commitment on volume or scheduling, we can look at sharpening the number in the next round. As the seller opening the negotiation, I want to anchor near the top of the visible market range while leaving some room to negotiate. This protects margin early and signals that any movement will need to be tied to concrete concessions.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.92, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.39.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for manufacturer_retailer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 3.54.

steps.message: Appreciate the opening position. At 2.92 you're very close to the top of what we can support on the retail side, so to get this moving I'd put forward 2.78 per unit as a more workable starting point. If you can show some flexibility around volume planning or delivery scheduling, we can keep narrowing the gap quickly. The seller opened just under our ceiling, so there is little room to accept immediately without testing for movement. A counter at 2.78 protects our margin and signals willingness to negotiate while leaving room for a follow-up move if needed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement. I can come down from 2.92, but 2.78 is still too light for us once production and fulfillment are accounted for. If we want to close this quickly, I can offer 2.86 per unit, and if you can give us steadier volume visibility or cleaner delivery scheduling, that helps justify the concession. The buyer improved from the seller's last offer but remains above the manufacturer's floor, so a counteroffer is the best move. Pricing at 2.86 preserves margin while signaling flexibility and keeping room for a final compromise in a later turn.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.86, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.39.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement from 2.92 to 2.86, and I want to keep this moving toward a close. We still need to stay disciplined on landed cost at retail, so I can improve to 2.82 per unit if that gets us over the line now. That narrows the gap meaningfully from our last position and reflects that we're trying to meet you halfway while keeping the program workable on our side. The seller's offer is below our maximum buy price, but there is still room to improve the economics before accepting. A modest counter at 2.82 signals seriousness, preserves buyer surplus, and leaves room for a final agreement within the acceptable range.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the move to 2.82 — that does help narrow the gap and shows you're trying to get this wrapped up. We still need to protect our production economics and downstream margin, but I can meet you partway at 2.84 per unit as a near-final position. That keeps the program viable on our side while giving you another concession from 2.86 to help close this out quickly. The buyer's 2.82 offer is above our floor, but there is little time left and a small counter can improve margin while preserving a strong chance of agreement. A move to 2.84 signals flexibility without giving up the remaining spread immediately.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.84, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.39.

steps.message: We can proceed at 2.84 per unit. That is close enough to our last position to get this finalized now, and I appreciate the movement from 2.86 to help close the remaining gap. Given the limited room left and the time pressure, this works for us and lets us move ahead without dragging out the last few cents. The seller's 2.84 offer is within our maximum buy price of 2.94 and only slightly above our prior 2.82 bid. With little time left in the negotiation, accepting secures supply at a workable price rather than risking losing the deal over a very small difference.
steps.outcome: Negotiation ends with an accepted deal.

negotiations.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
negotiations.dependency_note: Manufacturer sell floor uses the accepted upstream deal as its cost basis.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal accepted at 2.27 USD. Downstream deal accepted at 2.84 USD.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The manufacturer carried a cost basis of 2.27 USD, which forced a downstream sell floor of 2.65 USD.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
